message.content:
Synthesise the forensic evidence into a verdict on OUR central thesis.

OUR THESIS (about to be built): POLARIS scores 43.82 because its report is 12 paragraphs of 677 words with zero
subsections and lists facts rather than explaining them. Fix = subsections + ~80-word single-idea paragraphs +
cross-source reconciliation sentences (hedged, no new numbers/entities) + a dedicated Critical Synthesis section
+ more coverage (7.7k -> ~11-12k words).

THE PROBES:
[
 {
  "skill": "
",
  "ok": true,
  "findings": [
   "ANSWER TO THE KEY QUESTION: cellcog's 0 bullets / 0 tables on task 72 is GENRE ADAPTATION, not house style. Across 100 tasks cellcog's median is 38 bullets (EN) / 54 (ZH) and it goes as high as 286 bullets + 49 tables (task 39, game-genre user profiles). It emits ZERO bullets on exactly 5/100 tasks, and those 5 are all scholarly/essayistic: t72 lit review, t79 film paper, t82 history analysis, t45 Laozi commentary exegesis, t98. It uses tables on 94/100 tasks. => DO NOT ban bullets outright. Ban them for literature-review / scholarly-essay genres only.",
   "THE REAL DISCRIMINATOR IS BULLET DENSITY, NOT BULLET COUNT. Top-3 systems run 0.6-4.5 bullets per 1k words; bottom systems run 15-38. tavily packs 116 bullets into 2,960 words (38.2/kw). baidu-qianfan, the #1 system, uses MORE bullets in absolute terms (143 median) than tavily -- but spreads them over 36,194 words (4.2/kw). The bullet count on its own predicts nothing; bullets-per-1k-words separates the board cleanly.",
   "TABLES ARE A TOP-TIER MARKER. Top-3 (baidu 34, deepinsight 12.5, cellcog 4 median EN tables) use tables on nearly every task -- baidu has zero tasks without a table. Bottom-2 use essentially none: onyx has 0 tables on 86% of tasks, tavily on 99%. Tables survive the cleaner. This is a cheap, unexploited lever for POLARIS (currently 0 tables).",
   "GENRE ADAPTIVITY ITSELF CORRELATES WITH RANK. Applied-topic vs scholarly-topic bullet-density ratio: baidu 9.6x, cellcog 3.1x, deepinsight 1.5x (top 3, all adapt) vs onyx 1.4x, tavily 1.3x, gemini-ref 1.4x (bottom, flat house style). Coefficient of variation of bul/kw across tasks: baidu 1.03, cellcog 0.98, deepinsight 1.45 vs onyx 0.58, tavily 0.41, gemini-ref 0.39. The winners change shape per task; the losers apply one template to everything.",
   "POLARIS rank10 IS STRUCTURALLY PATHOLOGICAL AND OFF-DISTRIBUTION ON A DIMENSION NOBODY IS TRACKING. /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md body = 11 H2 sections, each ONE unbroken paragraph. 12 paragraphs total, median 679 words each (IQR 562-822), 20.2 sentences per paragraph, and ZERO H3 headings. The longest single line is 6,701 characters. Every corpus system sits at 45-235 median paragraph words and 1.3-8.4 sentences/para. POLARIS is 3x the worst paragraph size in the corpus and 2.4x the worst sentence density. This is a readability-dimension bomb (RACE scores readability separately) and it is invisible to the word-count/section-count levers the flywheel has been pulling.",
   "LENGTH IS NOT POLARIS'S BOTTLENECK; SHAPE IS. POLARIS rank10 = 7,767 words on task 72. Rank-1-on-this-board cellcog scored 56.67 with 11,027 words on the same task. POLARIS is already within 30% of a winning length. But cellcog splits its 11k words into 106 paragraphs under 32 H3 subheads; POLARIS dumps 7.7k words into 12 paragraphs under 0 H3s. Fixing paragraph granularity + adding H3s is free (no extra research, no extra tokens) and is the highest-leverage structural change available.",
   "H3 SUBHEADS ARE STANDARD AND POLARIS HAS NONE. EN medians: baidu 50 H3, cellcog 32, onyx 28, deepinsight 25, gemini-ref 21, tavily 8. POLARIS rank10: 0. Headings survive the cleaner and feed readability + instruction-following."
  ],
  "evidence": "METHOD VALIDATION (regex cleaner emulation vs. the real LLM-cleaned artifact on disk, claude-3-7-sonnet-latest, n=100 raw/cleaned pairs):
  word-count error: median 0.0%, mean +0.4%, p90 +1.3%
  tables:   exact match 100/100
  bullets:  exact match  94/100 (mean delta +0.44)
  headings: exact match  87/100
  => raw structure counts == cleaned structure counts. Structure numbers below are ground truth.

Scores from leaderboard_gemini25pro_judge.csv (only board covering all 5 on-disk artifacts).

=== Raw article size sanity check (median chars/doc, 100 docs) ===
baidu-qianfan  141,786  (max 697,602)
deepinsight     98,194  (max 353,269)
cellcog         44,382  (max 117,847)
onyx            32,696
tavily          23,368

Scripts: /tmp/structprof.py (profiler + validated cleaner emulation), /tmp/run.py, /tmp/genre.py, /tmp/genre2.py, /tmp/final.py",
  "surprises": [
   "CONTRADICTS 'ban bullets': the #1 system on this board (baidu-qianfan, 58.03) uses the MOST bullets of anyone (143 median EN, 64 even on task 72's literature review) and the most tables (34 median). Bullets are not penalized. cellcog's task-72 zero is a genre exception it makes on 5% of tasks, not a style.",
   "CONTRADICTS 'more sections is exhausted' (commit e87f52e): POLARIS has 11 H2 and 0 H3. The corpus median is 21-50 H3s. The unexplored axis is not MORE sections, it is SUBDIVISION and PARAGRAPH BREAKS inside existing sections. POLARIS's 12 paragraphs of 679 words each vs cellcog's 106 paragraphs of 96 words on the same task is the single largest structural gap in the whole dataset, and no flywheel rank has ever measured it.",
   "CONTRADICTS the length thesis: POLARIS at 7,767 words is NOT short. cellcog beats the whole board on task 72 with 11,027 words. But baidu wins overall with 36,194 EN median words / 141k chars per doc -- there are two viable strategies (tight-and-structured ~9-11k, or vast-and-tabular ~36k+), and POLARIS is executing neither. It is mid-length with zero internal structure, which is the worst of both.",
   "cellcog uses 0 bullets on t98 (non-alcoholic cocktail crafting techniques) -- an applied/how-to topic where the genre heuristic would predict bullets. So the adaptation is driven by the PROMPT'S REQUESTED OUTPUT FORM ('Research Topic / Objective / Investigate' framing = prose), not by the topic taxonomy label. A genre router should read the prompt's verb, not the subject area.",
   "The gemini-2.5-pro REFERENCE (the parity=0.50 anchor, 49.71) has 170 median EN bullets at 15.4 bullets/kw and near-zero adaptivity (CV 0.39). It is structurally a BOTTOM-tier artifact. Beating parity does not require beating a good report -- it requires beating a bulleted, unadaptive one. POLARIS at 43.82 is losing to a weak structural baseline, which means the deficit is not subtle."
  ]
 },
 {
  "ok": true,
  "findings": [
   "THE THESIS IS BACKWARDS. Winners do NOT trade grounding for ungrounded interpretation — they ship MORE of both. On the 50 EN tasks (citation markers + URLs stripped to approximate the RACE cleaner), cellcog (55.78) has 37% of sentences containing a number vs the reference's (49.98) 14%; 42.4% of cellcog sentences lack any number/proper-noun anchor vs 53.9% for the reference. cellcog is the MOST grounded system in the corpus AND the most interpretive. The reference is the vaguer document and it loses by 5.8 points.",
   "THE REAL DIFFERENTIATOR IS THE 'VERDICT SENTENCE': an unanchored, evaluative, cross-source judgment. cellcog ships 2.22 of these per 1000 words (6.4% of all sentences) vs reference 1.69, onyx 0.64, tavily 0.53. POLARIS ships 0.00–0.76 per 1000 words on task 72 — two of three POLARIS runs contain ZERO verdict sentences. This is the span-verification mechanism made visible: a sentence like 'The evidence does not support the claim that AI is causing uniform, industry-wide homogenization' has no source span, so POLARIS structurally cannot emit it. That is the insight deficit.",
   "POLARIS IS NOT UNDER-ATTRIBUTING OR UNDER-QUANTIFYING — IT ALREADY BEATS CELLCOG ON BOTH. On task 72, POLARIS rank12_rank10 has 2.03 narrative attributions per 1000 words vs cellcog's 1.43, and 57% of sentences contain a number vs cellcog's 37%. Do NOT spend effort adding named studies or numbers. The gap is elsewhere.",
   "NAMED-STUDY ATTRIBUTION IN RUNNING PROSE IS THE CLEANER-SAFE CHANNEL, AND CELLCOG OWNS IT. Narrative attributions ('Punyaleadtip et al. (2020) combined LSTM with SVR...') SURVIVE the cleaner — confirmed by 41 surviving instances in the already-cleaned reference.jsonl. cellcog: 1.43 narrative + 0.77 parenthetical per 1k words (2.20 total). Reference: 0.10 + 0.01 = 0.11. onyx: 0.35. tavily: 0.20. Meanwhile onyx and tavily push 92.5 and 77.1 bracket markers per 1k words — ALL DELETED. onyx post-clean is an unattributed assertion pile.",
   "WINNERS HEDGE LESS, NOT MORE. The reference uses 'potentially' 20.6x/10k words and 'might' 9.6x/10k; cellcog uses them 2.0x and 1.4x. cellcog's total hedge rate is 5.0/1k vs reference 7.1/1k and POLARIS 7.4–9.1/1k. POLARIS is the second-most-hedged system in the corpus. The proposed 'hedged, uncited, number-free contract' is a precise description of the REFERENCE, which scores 49.98. Do not ship it.",
   "CONFLICT IS RESOLVED BY INTRODUCING AN ANALYTICAL DISTINCTION, NOT BY AVERAGING OR LISTING. cellcog's canonical move (t72): name three studies with contradictory numbers, then write 'These are not contradictory findings; they are measuring different links in a causal chain.' cellcog runs 0.89 reconciliation moves per 10k words vs reference 0.37, onyx 0.26, tavily 0.30.",
   "MASSIVE STRUCTURAL GAP: cellcog carries 44–48 headings per report and 48 table rows/report; POLARIS carries 12–14 headings and ZERO tables. cellcog: 74% executive summary, 92% numbered sections, 54% limitations section, 40% future-research section, 24% table of contents. POLARIS: 0% exec summary, 0% numbered sections, 0% tables.",
   "CELLCOG COINS A NAMED ORGANIZING THESIS (0.44/report) AND PUTS IT IN THE TITLE: 'A Conditional Epistemic Amplifier' (t77), 'The Bifurcation Thesis' + 'The Distribution-First Inversion' (t93), 'Creative Stratification, Not Blanket Homogenization' (t87). The report has an argument, not a summary. Reference: 0.26/report. POLARIS: 0.",
   "CELLCOG STEELMANS THE OPPOSITION IN A DEDICATED SECTION. t77 §9 is literally titled 'The Case Against NFC's Importance', states five bulleted attacks on its own thesis ('The "conditional effects" argument risks unfalsifiability'), then rebuts. t87 §4.6 is titled 'Verdict:'. This is the highest-yield, lowest-risk insight structure: it is pure reasoning, requires no new sources, and cannot be span-verified — which is exactly why POLARIS never produces it.",
   "ACTIONABLE CONTRACT: POLARIS needs a bounded, explicitly-unverified VERDICT LAYER of roughly 2 sentences per 1000 words (~15–25 per report), confined to (a) an executive summary with a named thesis, (b) per-section 'what this means' verdicts, (c) a 'Case Against / Limitations' section, (d) a reconciliation sentence wherever two sources disagree. Everything else stays span-verified. This is ~5–6% of sentences — not a wholesale abandonment of grounding."
  ],
  "evidence": "MEASUREMENT BASIS: 50 English tasks (ids 51-100) from /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/{cellcog,onyx,tavily,baidu-qianfan-drs-pro,deepinsight}.jsonl and /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/third_party/deep_research_bench/data/test_data/cleaned_data/reference.jsonl. All metrics computed AFTER stripping [n]/[[n]](url) markers, bare URLs, and trailing reference sections (approximating utils/clean_article.py). Scripts: /tmp/rhet.py, /tmp/rhet2.py, /tmp/alt.py, /tmp/aud.py, /tmp/pol2.py.

=== TABLE 1: ATTRIBUTION AND GROUNDING (50 EN tasks) ===
system            avg_w  NARRattr/1k  PARENattr/1k  [n]markers/1k(DELETED)  %sent_with_number
cellcog (55.78)    9435      1.43         0.77            5.9                    37%
onyx               4237      0.35         0.02           92.5                    41%
tavily             3451      0.20         0.03           77.1                    44%
qianfan           40490      0.17         0.00           19.2                    23%
reference (49.98)  9107      0.10         0.01            0 (pre-cleaned)         14%
deepinsight       30862      0.05         0.01            0                        -
POLARIS t72 (rank12_rank10, cleaned): 7898w, NARRattr/1k = 2.03, %sent_with_number = 57%

CLEANER-SURVIVAL PROOF: grepping the ALREADY-CLEANED reference.jsonl for narrative author-year yields 41 surviving hits, e.g. t54: "Punyaleadtip et al. (2020) combined LSTM with Support Vector Regression (SVR) to generate views for BL." and "Almahdi and Yang (2017) used LSTMs to predict volatility for MV optimization." => narrative attribution is CLEANER-SAFE. Bracket markers are not.

=== TABLE 2: INTERPRETATION / VERDICT DENSITY (11 varied tasks: 58,62,67,72,75,77,82,87,90,93,97) ===
"no-anchor" = sentence has no digit and no non-initial capitalized token. "verdict" = no-anchor AND contains an evaluative/normative marker.
system          sents  %no-anchor  %eval  %no-anchor&eval  verdict/1k_words
cellcog          3602     42.4%     13.5%      6.4%             2.22
qianfan         13749     41.0%     12.6%      5.4%             1.81
reference        4184     53.9%      8.5%      4.4%             1.69
onyx              801     26.8%     18.2%      3.9%             0.64
tavily            516     24.6%     22.3%      3.9%             0.53

=== TABLE 3: HEAD-TO-HEAD ON TASK 72 (AI & labour-market literature review) ===
system                       words  verdict/1k  headings  hedge/1k
cellcog t72                  12597     1.67        44        6.9
reference t72                 8861     1.02        33       10.6
POLARIS rank12_rank10 t72     7898     0.76        12        8.1
POLARIS rank12_rank9  t72     6443     0.00        12        7.4
POLARIS pol_r4_themefloor t72 4212     0.00        14        8.5
(cellcog t90 = 2.65 verdict/1k, 48 headings; cellcog t87 = 2.17, 29 headings)

=== TABLE 4: STRUCTURE (% of 50 EN reports with the section) ===
sys        coined_frameworks/doc  %limitations  %future-research  %methodology  %synthesis  %execsum  %numbered  %TOC   tables/doc  bullets/doc
cellcog            0.44               54%            40%             54%           56%       74%       92%      24%      48.2        36.6
onyx               0.02               44%            10%             50%           34%        4%      100%       0%       1.4       119.3
tavily             0.00               32%            10%            100%           28%       54%       52%       0%       0.2       137.6
qianfan            2.46               74%            44%             66%           92%       18%        0%       0%     238.1       177.1
reference          0.26               30%            26%             20%           40%       20%        0%       0%      21.8        94.4
POLARIS (t72 runs)  0                  -              -               -             -         0%        0%       0%       0.0         0.0

=== TABLE 5: HEDGING VOCABULARY (occurrences per 10,000 words, 50 EN tasks) ===
cellcog:   may=12.9, approximately=6.9, could=4.6, uncertainty=3.1, suggests=2.7, likely=2.6, potentially=2.0, roughly=1.9, might=1.4, "not yet"=1.2
reference: potentially=20.6, may=11.6, might=9.6, could=5.8, likely=4.6, suggests=3.6, uncertainty=2.9
tavily:    uncertainty=4.5, may=4.5, likely=1.6, could=1.2, roughly=1.0
=> cellcog's top hedges are PRECISION hedges ("approximately", "roughly"); the reference's are EPISTEMIC-MUSH hedges ("potentially", "might"). Total hedge rate: cellcog 5.0/1k, reference 7.1/1k, POLARIS 7.4-9.1/1k.

=== TABLE 6: RECONCILIATION MOVES (per 10,000 words, 50 EN tasks) ===
cellcog 0.89 | qianfan 0.86 | reference 0.37 | tavily 0.30 | onyx 0.26

=== VERBATIM: HOW CELLCOG RECONCILES CONFLICTING EVIDENCE ===
[t72, the single best example - introduce an analytical distinction that dissolves the conflict, anchored on three named studies]
"A central organizing principle of this review is the analytical distinction between three levels at which AI's labor market effects can be measured: *exposure* ... *adoption* ... and *employment outcomes* ... This distinction matters because the three levels yield strikingly different pictures. Eloundou, Manning, Mishkin, and Rock (2024) estimated that approximately 80% of the U.S. workforce could see at least 10% of their tasks affected by large language models -- a measure of exposure. Yet Bick, Blandin, and Deming (2024) found that only 26.5% of U.S. workers were actually using generative AI at work by late 2024 -- a measure of adoption. And Humlum and Vestergaard (2025), using administrative records in Denmark, found precise null effects on earnings and recorded hours within two years of chatbot adoption -- a measure of employment outcomes. **These are not contradictory findings; they are measuring different links in a causal chain.** Much of the confusion and disagreement in public and scholarly discussion of AI and employment arises from conflating these levels."

[t77, reconciling theory-vs-evidence gap by adding a level of analysis]
"This ecological perspective explains Marchlewska's central finding at the societal level and reconciles the apparent contradiction between the theoretical prediction (NFC should matter substantially) and the empirical finding (NFC's average effect is small)."

[t77, naming a contradiction and REFUSING to resolve it - honest non-reconciliation]
"Strikingly, the high-belief profile exhibited **lower NFC** than the low- and moderate-belief profiles (means approximately 43.96 vs. 46.74/46.80). This directly contradicts the simple 'high closure -> more conspiracy' prediction and remains unexplained."

[t93, market-sizing conflict: EXPLAIN THE VARIANCE, don't average it]
"Published estimates range from $0.72 billion to $3.54 billion for 2025 depending on scope -- a nearly 5x variance that reflects genuinely different product categories bundled under a single label."

[t77, distinguishing absence-of-evidence from evidence-of-absence]
"The hypothesis remains theoretically motivated but **untestable with current data** -- a design gap, not evidence against the prediction."

=== VERBATIM: UNGROUNDED CROSS-SOURCE INFERENCE (no single source says this) ===
[t90 s7.4 "The Moral Hazard of Incremental Automation" - inference stated FIRST, then re-anchored to three named cases]
"A non-obvious economic dynamic drives the ADAS liability problem: manufacturers capture the economic upside of automation marketing (premium pricing, brand differentiation) while externalizing liability risk onto drivers who are told they must supervise a system that cognitive science says they cannot reliably supervise. This moral hazard is enabled by the SAE level system itself, which provides a technical classification (L2 = driver responsible) that manufacturers use as a legal shield while marketing the system as if it were substantially more autonomous. The Benavides verdict, the California DMV branding enforcement, and the ADAS class action represent the legal system beginning to close this moral hazard gap."

[t87 s4.6, heading is literally "### 4.6 Verdict: Creative Stratification, Not Blanket Homogenization" - REJECTS the prompt's framing and substitutes a sharper coined claim]
"The evidence does not support the claim that AI is causing uniform, industry-wide homogenization. It supports something more specific and, in many ways, more concerning: **creative stratification**. AI amplifies existing creative disparities. Designers with strong vision, established brand identity, and access to customized tools -- van Herpen, Kamali, Thompson -- use AI to push boundaries further and faster than they could alone. Designers without these advantages are pulled toward the algorithmic mean... The result is not a flattened landscape but a polarized one -- a 'hollowing out'"

[t77 s9 "The Case Against NFC's Importance" - dedicated steelman section, five bulleted self-attacks then rebuttal]
"Intellectual honesty requires confronting the strongest version of the skeptical position... **The 'conditional effects' argument risks unfalsifiability.** When NFC predicts misinformation, proponents cite it as confirmation. When it does not, they argue the conditions were not right. Without precise, pre-specified boundary conditions, this pattern of reasoning can accommodate any result... These criticisms have genuine force, and the report would be dishonest to minimize them. However, several rebuttals deserve consideration..."
"The honest conclusion is that NFC's importance depends on what question you are asking. For **theoretical understanding** ... For **practical prediction** ... For **intervention design** ..."

[t77 Executive Summary - a bolded blockquote THESIS, an original organizing concept in the title]
"> **Need for cognitive closure functions not as a direct driver of misinformation acceptance, but as a conditional epistemic amplifier -- a motivational disposition that magnifies vulnerability to misinformation under specific circumstances...**"
"The theory is elegant. The evidence, however, tells a more complicated story."

[t62] "The relationship between physical gate fidelity and QEC overhead is *highly nonlinear* -- and this nonlinearity is arguably the most important factor in the scaling equation."

=== WHAT "UNGROUNDED" LOOKS LIKE FOR THE LOSER (same metric, different altitude) ===
Random sample of reference no-anchor sentences -- these are TEXTBOOK EXPOSITION, not inference:
[t67] "Lyapunov functions can be used to prove stability and invariance within safe sets."
[t62] "The key operational feature is the ability to physically transport (shuttle) individual ions or small groups of ions between these different zones."
[t77] "The core of misinformation acceptance lies in the development or endorsement of a *belief* in the false claim."
Random sample of cellcog no-anchor sentences -- these are VERDICTS:
[t87] "The evidence does not support the claim that AI is causing uniform, industry-wide homogenization."
[t87] "Fashion should prepare for this eventuality by building the institutional infrastructure now."
[t90] "The **EU** has definitively resolved this question."
=> Same "unanchored" bucket, opposite altitude. The metric that separates winners is not ungroundedness, it is EVALUATIVE ALTITUDE.

=== HOW LOSERS ATTRIBUTE (post-cleaner damage) ===
onyx t77 raw: "It can be **trait-like** ... and also **state-like** [[1]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8637961/), [[4]](http://fitelson.org/current/seizing.pdf)."
=> after cleaning: "It can be trait-like and also state-like." Zero attribution. onyx pushes 92.5 markers/1k words and loses ALL of them.
The reference leaves literal artifacts behind: "[Insight 6.1]", "[Insight 8.1]" survive into cleaned text in t77 s9.
The reference also asserts, confidently and (per cellcog's r=.09 meta-analytic figure) wrongly: "The evidence consistently indicates that NFC ... functions as a significant psychological factor increasing individual susceptibility." cellcog is precise where the reference is confidently vague.",
  "surprises": [
   "POLARIS ALREADY OUT-ATTRIBUTES AND OUT-QUANTIFIES CELLCOG. On task 72: POLARIS 2.03 narrative attributions/1k words vs cellcog 1.43; POLARIS 57% of sentences contain a number vs cellcog 37%. The hypothesis that we lose on insight because we lack named studies or grounded facts is FALSE. We have more of both than the #1 system. Any plan to 'add more named-study attributions' is wasted effort.",
   "THE PROPOSED REMEDY ('hedged, uncited, number-free contract') IS AN EXACT DESCRIPTION OF THE REFERENCE, WHICH SCORES 49.98 AND LOSES TO CELLCOG BY 5.8 POINTS. The reference has 0.11 named attributions/1k, 14% of sentences with numbers, 53.9% unanchored sentences, and hedges with 'potentially' 20.6x per 10k words. Shipping that contract would move POLARIS TOWARD the reference, not past it. The winner is the LEAST hedged (5.0/1k vs reference 7.1 vs POLARIS 7.4-9.1) and the MOST numerate document in the corpus.",
   "'UNGROUNDED INTERPRETATION' AS A VOLUME METRIC ANTI-CORRELATES WITH SCORE. cellcog has FEWER unanchored sentences (42.4%) than the reference (53.9%). The winning quantity is not the fraction of ungrounded sentences but their ALTITUDE: 15% of cellcog's unanchored sentences are evaluative verdicts vs 8% of the reference's. The reference's unanchored sentences are textbook filler ('Lyapunov functions can be used to prove stability'); cellcog's are judgments ('The evidence does not support the claim that AI is causing uniform homogenization').",
   "TWO OF THREE POLARIS RUNS CONTAIN LITERALLY ZERO VERDICT SENTENCES ON TASK 72 (verdict/1k = 0.00). Not 'few' - zero. Span-verification is not merely suppressing insight at the margin; it is a hard structural ban on the exact sentence type that separates 55.78 from 49.98. The required dose is small and boundable: cellcog runs 1.67-2.65 verdict sentences per 1000 words, i.e. ~6% of sentences, ~15-25 per report.",
   "STRUCTURE MAY BE AS BIG A LEVER AS INSIGHT AND IT IS FREE. cellcog carries 44-48 headings and 48 table rows per report; POLARIS carries 12-14 headings and ZERO tables. cellcog: 74% exec summary, 92% numbered sections. POLARIS: 0% and 0%. Tables and headings SURVIVE the cleaner intact. Prior flywheel work concluded 'more sections is exhausted on this corpus' (commit e87f52e) - but that was tested at 12-14 headings against a winner running 44-48. The section-count ceiling was never actually approached.",
   "onyx and tavily are CITATION-CHANNEL SUICIDES: they push 92.5 and 77.1 bracket markers per 1000 words, every one of which the ArticleCleaner deletes, leaving unattributed assertion piles. They are NOT models to copy on attribution. Only cellcog routes attribution through the cleaner-safe narrative channel. This means the leaderboard's citation-heavy mid-pack systems are being judged on text far weaker than what they shipped - and that a system that moves attribution into prose gets a free, uncontested advantage."
  ]
 },
 {
  "ok": true,
  "findings": [
   "CORPUS FRAMING IS WRONG: only cellcog of the 5 named artifacts (cellcog, tavily, onyx, deepinsight, baidu-qianfan) is on the GPT-5.5 board. The other 4 exist ONLY on the legacy gemini-2.5-pro board. The requested 'correlate 5 artifacts against GPT-5.5 scores' join is n=1, not n=5.",
   "BUT: /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/gpt55_board/ contains PER-TASK RACE scores for 9 systems (scores/*/raw_results.jsonl, 100 tasks each) PLUS their article JSONLs. That is 898 (system,task) observations on our ACTUAL evaluator, which supports task+system fixed effects. I used this instead of n=5. cellcog-max has aggregate-only scores (no raw_results.jsonl), hence 9 not 10 systems.",
   "CLAIM (d) IS DEAD ON ARRIVAL - THE PREMISE IS A FILE BUG. deepinsight.jsonl is DOUBLE-ESCAPED: 0 real newlines, 266 literal backslash-n two-char sequences. Unescaped it has ~58 headings and ~24 H3 per article, med_para ~117w. The '24,333-word single paragraph with zero headings' document DOES NOT EXIST. It was a JSONL escaping artifact read by a naive extractor. Our 'strongest available test of the central hypothesis' was measuring a parser bug.",
   "STRUCTURE THESIS DOES NOT SURVIVE FIXED EFFECTS. sections vs overall: +0.72 within-task (across systems), +0.69 after partialling out words, but +0.05 under 2-way (task+system) FE. h3: +0.57 -> +0.05. The between-system signal is the strongest in the whole feature battery and it survives the length control; the within-system marginal effect is zero. Within/between variation ratio for sections is 0.69, so the FE null is reasonably powered, not merely noise. This is the exact signature of structure being a SYMPTOM of a good research process rather than a CAUSE of a good score. The corpus CANNOT distinguish these.",
   "TABLES ARE THE ONLY FEATURE THAT SURVIVES FE. table_rows_per_1k: +0.18 with overall, +0.22 with readability under 2-way FE; stable in both EN (+0.18/+0.23) and ZH (+0.16/+0.20). Every other structural feature collapses to |rho| <= 0.07. If any single formatting lever is real, it is tables, not headings.",
   "CLAIM (b) 'LENGTH DRIVES COMPREHENSIVENESS' IS FALSE ABOVE A FLOOR. Task-adjusted mean overall by word bin: <2000w = -7.69; 2000-3500 = -0.89; 3500-5000 = +2.78 (PEAK); 5000-8000 = +2.16; 8000-12000 = +2.20; 12000-20000 = +1.69; 20000+ = -1.12. Length is a THRESHOLD, not a driver. Between systems, words|sections = +0.12 (nothing) while sections|words = +0.89. onyx at 4,554 words is not an anomaly needing explanation - it sits in the PEAK bin. Our flywheel push from 3,408 -> 7,723 -> 8,055 words was climbing a FLAT part of the curve.",
   "CLAIM (c) 'BULLETS HURT' IS FALSE - IT IS COINCIDENTAL. bullets_per_1k: -0.01 pooled, -0.01 within-task, -0.03 under 2-way FE, and +0.11 once length is partialled out (POSITIVE). For readability specifically it is +0.23 at fixed length. bodhi (21.1 bullets/1k) is #2 and dalpha (24.9 bullets/1k) has the HIGHEST readability of all 9 systems. tavily is simply a short, weak system (1,961-3,577w); its bullets are not the mechanism.",
   "CLAIM (a) 'SHORT PARAGRAPHS + MANY H3 => HIGHER READABILITY AND INSIGHT' IS NOT ESTABLISHED. It holds between systems only in ABSOLUTE counts (h3|words = +0.61 insight, +0.51 readability; med_para|words = -0.41 insight, -0.49 readability) - i.e. exactly the confounded direction. The NORMALIZED version (headings_per_1k) is near-zero raw (+0.14 readability, -0.02 insight) and NEGATIVE under FE (-0.10 overall). Under FE the whole claim vanishes: med_para -> -0.07 readability, -0.00 insight; h3 -> +0.03/+0.05. Counter-examples: perplexity has the HIGHEST heading density (11.3/1k) and is 8th of 9; grok has 8.3/1k and is last; dalpha has the highest readability with only 28 sections.",
   "THE FOUR RACE DIMENSIONS ARE ONE FACTOR. Inter-dimension r = 0.78-0.97 at (system,task) level and 0.95-1.00 at system level on both boards. insight vs comprehensiveness r=0.92 (task) / 0.99 (system). 'Optimize for insight, not comprehensiveness' is NOT an available lever. Readability is the most distinct (r=0.78-0.85) but also the lowest-variance dimension (sd 3.45 vs insight 6.56) - the points live in insight/comprehensiveness.",
   "HEDGING IS A STRONG NEGATIVE and is not currently one of our levers: hedge_per_1k = -0.39 within-task, -0.43 partialling out words, consistently negative across ALL dimensions. grok hedges at 8.3/1k and finishes last. (Under FE it collapses to -0.03, so it too is likely a quality proxy - but it is worth an intervention test alongside structure.)",
   "METHOD BUG I CAUGHT IN MY OWN CODE: my first paragraph extractor dropped any blank-line block that STARTED with a heading (several systems put heading+body in one block) and counted '---' rules as zero-word paragraphs. This manufactured a fake counter-example ('bodhi ships 312-word paragraphs and ranks #2'). Bodhi's true MEDIAN paragraph is 32 words; the 320 mean came from 3 blob-articles, one with a 10,796-word paragraph. Any med_para/sent_per_para number computed with a block-level extractor should be distrusted.",
   "RECOMMENDATION: do NOT build the wave on the structure thesis as stated. Run the one decisive cheap experiment first: take a fixed set of POLARIS reports, RE-RENDER THE IDENTICAL CONTENT at 2-3 structure levels (heading density, paragraph length, table count), re-judge with the GPT-5.5 RACE harness, read the delta. That converts an unfalsifiable observational correlation into a causal number in hours. Prioritize the TABLE arm - it is the only feature with FE support."
  ],
  "evidence": "SETUP
- Judge boards: /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/leaderboard_gpt55_judge.csv (10 rows), leaderboard_gemini25pro_judge.csv (45 rows).
- Overlap of the 5 named artifacts with the GPT-5.5 board: cellcog -> cellcog-max. deepinsight -> ABSENT. onyx -> ABSENT. tavily -> ABSENT. baidu-qianfan -> ABSENT.

- Scripts: /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/feat.py (cleaner-emulator + feature extractor), panel.py (FE panel), decisive.py (partials, variation budget, length bins), panel.json (cached 898-row panel).
- All features measured on CLEANED text (citation markers, md links, bare URLs, [citation:N], CJK bracket cites and trailing reference sections stripped), per the RACE ArticleCleaner contract.

Note perplexity: HIGHEST heading density (11.3/1k) of all 9, 8th of 9 on readability-adjacent overall. Direct counter-example to density-as-lever.

Answer honestly:
- Does the artifact data SUPPORT the structure thesis, or is structure merely CORRELATED with systems that are
  better in other ways? (deepinsight's single 24k-word paragraph is the sharpest test — what does it score?)
- What in our plan is now WRONG or unsupported?
- What did we MISS entirely?
- What can the artifacts NOT tell us (i.e. where must we run our own experiment)?

An honest "the data cannot distinguish these hypotheses" is more useful than a confident story.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll do a couple of quick spot-checks on the load-bearing forensic claims before synthesizing, since the verdict hinges on them.

input.command:
python3 -c "
import json
line = open('/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/deepinsight.jsonl').readline()
d = json.loads(line)
art = d.get('article') or d.get('content') or str(d)
print('real newlines:', art.count(chr(10)))
print('literal backslash-n:', art.count('\\\\n'))
"

python3 -c "
import re
body = open('/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md').read()
body = body.split('## References')[0]
h2 = len(re.findall(r'^## ', body, re.M))
h3 = len(re.findall(r'^### ', body, re.M))
paras = [p for p in re.split(r'\n\s*\n', body) if len(p.split())>10 and not p.strip().startswith('#')]
import statistics
wl = sorted(len(p.split()) for p in paras)
print('H2:', h2, 'H3:', h3, 'paras:', len(paras), 'median words:', statistics.median(wl) if wl else 0)
print('longest line chars:', max(len(l) for l in body.splitlines()))
print('tables:', len(re.findall(r'^\|', body, re.M)), 'bullets:', len(re.findall(r'^\s*[-*] ', body, re.M)))
"
input.description: Verify POLARIS rank10 report structure counts

content.content:
H2: 11 H3: 0 paras: 12 median words: 677.0
longest line chars: 6701
tables: 0 bullets: 0

input.what_the_data_says: DIAGNOSIS CONFIRMED, CAUSATION NOT, FIX SPEC PARTLY WRONG. (1) The pathology is real and verified on disk: /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md = 11 H2 / 0 H3 / 12 paragraphs / median 677 words per paragraph / 0 tables / 0 bullets / longest line 6,701 chars. Every corpus system runs 45-235 median paragraph words and 21-50 H3s; POLARIS is 3x beyond the worst observed value on a dimension (paragraph granularity) no flywheel rank ever measured. (2) But the causal structure claim fails its strongest available test: on 898 (system,task) observations with the actual GPT-5.5 RACE judge, sections go from rho +0.72 within-task to +0.05 under task+system fixed effects (h3: +0.57 -> +0.05; med_para -> -0.01), and the FE null is powered (within/between variation ratio 0.69 for sections). This is exactly the signature of structure as a SYMPTOM of a good research process rather than a cause of score. The one formatting feature that survives FE is TABLES (table_rows_per_1k: +0.18 overall, +0.22 readability, stable across EN/ZH) — which our plan omits entirely. The sharpest planned observational test is dead: deepinsight's '24k-word single paragraph' was a JSONL double-escaping artifact (verified: 0 real newlines, 266 literal \n in task 1); unescaped, deepinsight is heavily structured (~58 headings, 117w paragraphs). (3) The 'lists facts rather than explains' half is directionally right but our remedy is inverted. POLARIS already BEATS the #1-on-board system on grounding: 2.03 narrative attributions/1k vs cellcog 1.43, and 57% of sentences contain a number vs cellcog 37%. The measured deficit is the un-hedged evaluative VERDICT sentence (POLARIS 0.00-0.76/1k on task 72, two of three runs at literally zero, vs cellcog 1.67-2.65), plus reconciliation-by-analytical-distinction (cellcog 0.89/10k vs reference 0.37), a named organizing thesis (cellcog 0.44/report, POLARIS 0), exec summaries (74% vs 0%), and a dedicated 'Case Against' steelman section. Winners hedge LESS: cellcog 5.0/1k vs reference 7.1 vs POLARIS 7.4-9.1 (already second-most-hedged in the corpus), and hedge_per_1k is -0.39 within-task with the real judge. Our 'hedged, no new numbers/entities' reconciliation contract is a precise description of the 49.98-scoring reference. (4) Length is a floor, not a driver: task-adjusted overall peaks at 3,500-5,000 words (+2.78) and is flat from 5k to 12k (+2.16 to +2.20); the 7.7k->11-12k push climbs a flat curve. (5) The four RACE dimensions are one factor (r 0.78-0.97 at task level), so 'optimize insight specifically' is not an available lever. (6) POLARIS at 43.82 is losing to a structurally weak parity anchor (the gemini reference is bottom-tier on adaptivity and bulleted at 15.4/kw), so the deficit is not subtle — but the artifacts alone cannot tell us which fix closes it.
corrections_to_our_plan: DROP 'hedged' from the reconciliation-sentence spec. Hedging is negatively correlated with score everywhere it is measurable (-0.39 within-task on the GPT-5.5 judge), POLARIS is already the second-most-hedged system in the corpus (7.4-9.1/1k vs winner 5.0/1k), and the 'hedged, no new numbers, no new entities' contract exactly describes the reference artifact that scores 49.98 and loses by ~6 points. Reconciliation sentences should be declarative verdicts that introduce an analytical distinction ('These are not contradictory findings; they are measuring different links in a causal chain'), using precision hedges (approximately/roughly) only, not epistemic-mush hedges (potentially/might).

DEPRIORITIZE the 7.7k->11-12k word push. Length is a threshold, not a driver: task-adjusted score is flat from 5,000 to 12,000 words (+2.16 vs +2.20). POLARIS at 7.7k is already above the floor. Spend the token budget on tables and the verdict layer instead.

DO NOT add more named studies, numbers, or grounded facts. POLARIS already out-attributes (2.03 vs 1.43 narrative attributions/1k) and out-quantifies (57% vs 37% sentences with numbers) cellcog on task 72. Any 'more grounding' work is wasted; the gap is evaluative altitude, not evidence density.

ADD TABLES — the single omission with the strongest causal support. table_rows_per_1k is the ONLY feature surviving task+system fixed effects (+0.18 overall, +0.22 readability, stable EN and ZH). Top-3 systems table on nearly every task; POLARIS ships zero. This should be a first-class lever, arguably ahead of H3 subdivision.

REFRAME the Critical Synthesis section as a bounded VERDICT LAYER, not one section: ~2 verdict sentences per 1,000 words (~15-25 per report, ~5-6% of sentences), placed in (a) an executive summary with a NAMED organizing thesis in the title, (b) per-section 'what this means' verdicts, (c) a dedicated 'Case Against'/limitations steelman section, (d) reconciliation-by-distinction wherever sources disagree. Everything else stays span-verified. Note span-verification is currently a hard structural ban on this sentence type — two of three POLARIS runs contain ZERO verdict sentences — so this needs an explicit carve-out in the pipeline, not a prompt nudge.

TREAT the H3/paragraph fix as a hypothesis to test causally, not an established win. Under task+system FE its marginal effect is ~zero within the corpus's support region; the honest argument for it is that POLARIS is 3x outside that region (677w paragraphs vs corpus max ~235). It is free and cannot plausibly hurt, so ship it, but do not attribute expected points to it without the re-render experiment.

GENRE-GATE formatting rather than banning bullets: the #1 system (baidu-qianfan) uses the MOST bullets (143 median EN) and wins; bullets_per_1k is +0.11 on score at fixed length. The discriminator is bullet DENSITY adaptivity (winners 3-10x scholarly-vs-applied ratio, losers ~1.3x), and the router should read the prompt's requested output form (verb framing), not the topic label — cellcog goes zero-bullet on an applied cocktail-crafting task because the prompt asked for prose.
open_questions: THE CENTRAL UNRESOLVED QUESTION the artifacts cannot answer: does restructuring IDENTICAL content change the RACE score? Structure-as-cause vs structure-as-symptom are observationally indistinguishable in this corpus (FE kills sections/h3/med_para; between-system correlation is +0.89 even controlling for words). The decisive experiment is cheap: re-render a fixed set of POLARIS reports at 2-3 structure levels (H3 density, paragraph length, table count — prioritize the table arm) with content held constant, re-judge with the GPT-5.5 RACE harness, read the delta. Hours, not days.

Does the FE null even apply to POLARIS? Fixed effects estimate marginal effects within the corpus's observed range (paragraphs 45-235 words); POLARIS sits at 677. Fixing a 3x-off-distribution pathology may pay even where marginal structure variation does not. Only the re-render experiment resolves this.

Which judge/board is authoritative for the wave? Probe 1's headline numbers (cellcog 56.67, POLARIS 43.82, task-72 table) are from the LEGACY gemini-2.5-pro board; only cellcog of the 5 named artifacts exists on the GPT-5.5 board. If we're scored by GPT-5.5, every task-72 head-to-head number in our plan needs re-basing before we claim expected point gains.

Can the pipeline actually emit verdict sentences? Span-verification structurally bans them (no source span exists for 'the evidence does not support X'). Where in the compose/verify pipeline does the carve-out live, and how do we bound it so the hallucination risk stays at ~5-6% of sentences?

Do verdict-layer and structure gains stack, or are both proxies for the same latent 'good process' factor? cellcog has both; no system in the corpus has one without the other. A 2x2 (structure fix x verdict layer) on the re-render set would answer this.

What is the hedging intervention worth? hedge_per_1k is -0.39 within-task but collapses to -0.03 under FE (likely also a quality proxy). POLARIS's 7.4-9.1/1k is high enough that a de-hedging pass is worth one experimental arm, but no expected value should be booked from observational data.

Is 20k+ words actively penalized or just unsampled? The 20000+ bin goes negative (-1.12) on n=4 — too thin to rule out the baidu 'vast-and-tabular' strategy as an alternative to 'tight-and-structured'.
